title: Day in the life of a Denture Lab Technician
transcript: [Music] welcome to the day in the life of a dental lab technician at Aspen Dental this is our wonderful office that is located in Bourbonnais Illinois making our way into the lab through steel station where we stopped to turn on air water and Vac every morning then moving into the lab to turn on the lights put on any apron or proper PPE for the day we then start the day off by checking our schedule and emails to ensure that I am productive throughout the day with the things I do filling all Waters and adding candy to ensure proper sanitation throughout the day then soaking all models in the water for the desired time once the models are hydrated and trimmed I then will proceed to articulate any unarticulated cases and making sure my standard presentation is met for not only myself but as well as my patient after all the cases are proper then start working on my daily schedule the final step of every day is to buff and polish all dentures that were finished that day this is where we get that nice gloss all over next thing I do every day is an assessment with my trainee Amanda to make sure we are on track to meet all our goals I am very thankful for the support that I received from Aspen when it comes to training any new employees at the end of my schedule every day we are allotted 45 minutes for cleanup that will consist of scraping sweeping all floors dumping all Waters and cleaning your station for the next morning and to end the day of course we always change all our PPE dump our Waters and turn off air and water back just to start the next day at Aspen at the dental lab technician

title: Dental Laboratory Technicians
transcript: dental laboratory technicians design and create crowns bridges dentures and other prosthetics from impressions taken of a patient's mouth using molds supplied by dentists these technicians shape wax teeth that will be used as a model to cast a metal framework for the prosthetic device they then apply layers of color matched porcelain to the metal to create an exact replica of the patient's original tooth or teeth the majority of dental laboratory technicians learn their craft on the job some courses may be available at vocational schools and community colleges the necessary qualifications are manual dexterity good vision and the ability to recognize fine differences in color and shape most dental laboratory technicians never meet the patient who will use their creations but their skill at doing their jobs directly affects not only the physical and dental health of another person but also that person's confidence and self-esteem and that makes this a special job indeed

with one point solutions an end-to-end provider of laboratory furniture design manufacturing and installation to make this video we asked for expert advice to hear about dental laboratory business first hand first you need to settle on your dental niche to do that you could look at the latest trends in the dental industry on the websites such as lmtmag.com or by attending trade shows or following the journal of dental technology some niche examples are crown and bridge veneers dental sleep medicine implants or provisionals it all depends on how you can match your expertise with the available market one of our experts mentioned that being a full-service dental lab provider will help you address a wider range of service requests from dentists in theory making you the only one that they can rely on next select the right team to hire the best talent you could look up dental lab technicians in your area on linkedin where you can also advertise your job posting to recruit talent here's some expert advice on how to recruit the best talent while fine motor skills are virtually a prerequisite for a position technicians should be able to perform a variety of tasks in the lab early on before specializing on a niche one of the ways to evaluate a dental lab technician is to let them carve a chalk cast based on a given dental diagram when it comes to scale of your company consider the size of the market that you will serve how many dental clinics practitioners and patients there are in the area existing competition and what is your competitive advantage or unique value proposition together these factors will drive how you can better position yourself and set the optimal growth trajectory for your business which brings me to the budget the upfront costs to keep in mind are for property expenditures equipment and materials purchases and hiring personnel the scale that you have settled on will dictate what property area you will need and how many people you will need to hire the equipment and consumables are another big expenditure knowing the most reputable vendors on the market and intricacies of supply chain logistics for materials required in day-to-day operations are vital for early success of your company in addition current market conditions have impacted the supply chain of various consumables thus challenging existing dental lab businesses to meet their orders on time last but not least is designing your dental lab space at this point a few things need to be considered one are official regulations and laws two the actual design specifications in other words how to make sure to maximize utilization of space without compromising on comfort safety and efficiency one practical consideration from my experts is minimizing impact of working with particulate substances on nearby surfaces and outlets and another is to provide easily accessible waste disposal bins near each station this is where one point solutions comes in our experts can help you in this process and guide you every step of the way from design to manufacturing to installation we offer short lead times competitive pricing and products that are made here in aaction and efficiency delivering beautiful lifelike protheses faster than ever contrary to some information found online photogrammetry is not a 3D scanner instead it functions more like a camera that captures images of individually calibrated scan Flex in the patient's mouth these images are used to calculate the spatial relationships in distances between multi-unit abutments however it does not determine the exact coordinates of these multi-units but rather there relative positions to each other each scan flag is unique and must be assigned to a specific position in the patient's mouth therefore meticulous preparation and organization are essential beforehand otherwise the entire measurement may be inaccurate all the scan flags are positioned onto the multi-units in the patient's mouth in the order they were initially organized on the tray hand tighten them and tilt the flex so that all the dots are visible from a straight view using retractors to open the patient's mouth we then use a photogramm camera to capture the position of all the flags the position is then output as an sdl file reflecting the specific situation in the patient's mouth the challenging part is now organizing and aligning all the different scans first we have a photogramm file as an STL second we have the pre-up scan before the surgery third we have an antagonist in bite relation with the preup fourth we have a pre-up scan with markers and I usually play three on the lower Arch fifth we have the post surgery scan with white caps same as a photogrametry file and the scan markers in the pallet now in exate we need to import all the scans in the software it is a good practice to number and rename the files in the order exocad prompts for their import the first step is to align all the scans should be straightforward as it is initially the same situation next we align the post surgery scan the one with the markers of the pallet to the preup scan the one with the markers in the pallet for the lower Arch this can be tricky so I recommend placing three markers two in the posterior region and one in the anterior region I use these scan markers as my primary alignment points finally I align the photogrammetry sdl to the post surgery skin if the alignment is significantly off I would shake the fit of of the scan marker scans in the patient's mouth and take a new photogrametry image usually these scans align really well especially when you're working with excellent oil surgeons like Dr O'Neal and Dr simonon from Harbor Light in Ohio once aligned I set the orientation exocad and clean up the postsurgery tissue scan and under edit mesh I close for example holes and remove artifacts then using free form scan data I gently smooth out the scan as a tissue is still very soft and areas next carefully press the material into the interproximal area clay modeling instruments with silicone tips work great for this step I wet the tip with model LC from nx10 and apply slight pressure to push easy gum between the teeth then with a swift motion I smooth out the basil area ensuring it thins out nicely be sure no material gets into the multi-unit interfaces because it will be out areas if needed make sure to clean up any easy gum on the te's before curing them as it will be harder to remove once it's set after light curing easy gum for about 3 minutes I use Diamond disc title: What's in Your Mouth? -- The Issue
transcript: [Music] because a dentist prescription is legally required for a dental laboratory to begin any project patients cannot access dental laboratory services directly this disconnect between the patient and the dental lab can hinder the dissemination of critical information such as where the restorations are produced or what materials they contain growing demand for dental work in america has created a market that features both high-end and economy-priced work quantities of dental restorations are increasingly being imported from countries like china pakistan and vietnam where the dental laboratories are not subject to the same scrutiny as domestic laboratories are by the u.s food and drug administration even domestic dental labs remain unregulated in over 40 of the state dental practice acts in fact no state in the u.s requires dental technicians to be licensed of the 7 billion spent annually on dental restorations in the u.s an estimated 40 are important the fda requires dental laboratories that ship work overseas to inform their dentist through proper labeling however there is no mandate that dennis passed this information on to the patient and so many of them don't some laboratories even neglect to inform their dentists who in turn unknowingly place foreign products into patients mouths there are some dental labs in the us that do not produce any of their own work they can bear deceptive patriotic sounding names and exist essentially as fronts through which cheaply made foreign dental products are funneled to dentists for very low prices poorly made dental restorations whether in america or abroad can lead to a range of health consequences for patients and in turn legal consequences for dentists [Music]

transcript: [Music] if you've been paying attention to the 3d printing world you might know that dental labs are among the most advanced users of 3d printing out there to walk us through this landscape to give us some insight into how dental users employ 3d printing we have jamie stover who's carbon's senior manager of dental lab applications jamie it's great to have you with us ah thank you it's great to be here i'll i'll show them anywhere where uh experts in the title and i can be a considered one so this is why we have you so tell us a little bit about how 3d printing is used in dental applications well you know surprisingly there's there are quite a few the most widely used applications are models uh printed models for cases originating from intraoral scanners another uh pretty popular application is surgical guides so printing surgical guides for cad cam guided implant surgery one of the latest and probably the most impactful that we'll probably spend a little bit of time talking about here today is printed dentures and night guards dentures and night guards are traditionally done from acrylic it's a very labor intensive process and being able to print those applications is been a game changer in our industry yeah tell us a little bit about what 3d printing is replacing what did these processes look like before they got digitized the dental app industry has it's really undergone a couple of digital transformations and the first digital transformation was a decade or so ago and it changed the way that the fixed side of the industry fabricated restorations and by fixed i'm referring to permanent restorations like crowns or bridges that are cemented into the patient's mouth and then the second wave of digital transformation of our industry is actually happening currently um it's changing the way that the removable restoration such as dentures and night guards are fabricated and like i said earlier you know traditionally these are these prosthesis are fabricated by skilled technicians who perform many many labor intensive steps by hand and it's time consuming expensive and it's really impossible to scale so the development of new resins for these applications is it's allowing us in labs and in the lab industry to transfer the fabrication to you know digital design software and an additive you know 3d printing technology it's really bringing improved production throughput and i mean positive economic impacts from the reduction of labor required tell us a little bit about what the 3d printing technologies need to be able to accomplish to be successful in dental labs you know if you think about the fact that each restoration a dental lab fabricates it's a bespoke product i mean meaning it it's um it's a one-off completely custom product that only fits in one patient's mouth one way and dental apps have to do this at scale and they also have to be profitable and there's also relationships they have to manage with their clients these products they must also be you know biocompatible fda approved to be safe for use in a patient's mouth and they need to be strong and aesthetic so a lot of demands i mean the challenges are endless and 3d printing is such an efficient and accurate production method it allows labs to check all of these boxes for their production a real challenge in the dental lab industry and one that i'm very familiar with is hardware obsolescence it's a major hurdle for labs the typical in use life is less than you know two to three years for most of this equipment carbon subscription model provides we provide smart hardware really it's it's constantly improving over time with software updates so the printer you get on day one is actually better a year later carbon printers are also proactively monitored and we can identify components that may fail and replace them before they do so unplanned downtime is almost non-existent when you're transitioning from analog to digital processes you're moving people into those processes and the applications that you're producing and providing to your dentist and the patients they change so if that equipment goes down it's a it's a workflow stoppage and it's it's absolutely devastating another major uh advantage and impact this is having is actually a digital record so once a lab does a digital printed denture they store that design file as a record and so if something happens to that denture they can just pull that file up and reproduce it very quickly without the patient even having to go back to the dentist so what digital production of dentures allows if we just focus the lens on that product it allows less appointments for the initial denture you can cut two to three appointments out so you go from six appointments for that patient to maybe three or four and then once it's created you have a record on file the patient doesn't have to go back to the dentist something that really really excites me because being in the industry for so long and seeing this transition to get to this point is pretty something i'm pretty proud of and happy about so if viewers want to learn a little bit more about title: How digital workflows save my lab time and costs
transcript: [Music] I'm chair of state I'm the founder of DTL medium and that's a full digital app and we settled in here for artists at town close to Emerson in our laboratory is fully digital lab we offer all kind of cases so we're doing input cases crown and bridges dentures and orthodontics we work with eight dental technician now my advice for labs who want to start in a digital workflow is always start full digital start today did cause a lot of time to produce something so for example making a denture from the beginning to the end would cost up to one working date to produce it must saves a lot of time because we can make a dent in out from in two to two-and-a-half hours working time we can count on a really high standard of quality because of the accurate tree shaped scanners on every step of the workflow we can go back into the design onto manufacturing process and we can reproduce or do a correction on any case we can use now a 2d or a 3d picture into the software and use it into the design so we can copy the kind of the form of the tea for the patient and we can bring it back into the dentures about 30 to 40 percent of our cases are into our scans so that's also a big benefit of working digital we can work with the digital dentist experience where the software is really great because there are a lot of automated and guided workflows in the software but it also has to pull the possibility to be more creative if you want if you get more familiar with a system and you get to know the idea behind it the software you will be amazed which you can do it as powerful something we are really more productive because labor cost was around 50% of our total cost on an analog lab now thanks to the tree-shaped dental system our labor costs have dropped down to 20% of our total cost [Music] [Music]

title: The Digital Workflow Explained
transcript:
Is it time to move to a digital workflow?
With DDX enabled the process is simple. See how digital can benefit your
workflow. When the patient arrives at the dental office, the first step in the
ConnectDental Digital Solutions process is to take a digital impression. Then the
dental office can send the impression to the laboratory using the Dentrix
practice management system to DDX's web-based application. DDX improves the
workflow process by allowing the lab and the practice to communicate more
effectively and efficiently. Once received the lab reviews the impression
and case information. Then using the digital information received through DDX,
the technician begins to design the dental restoration. After digitally
designing the restoration, it is time to choose the restorative material and
manufacture the restoration. Today's dentists and laboratory technicians can
choose from a wide selection of highly translucent material objects such as
Zirlux Zirconia as well as other high-strength esthetic restorative
solutions. Once manufactured, the next step in characterizing the restoration
is to give it a natural look and feel. Then it is packaged and sent back to the
dental practice. Once received by the practice, the final step in this workflow
is for the dentist to seat the finished restoration. Fast, efficient, and
consistent. Today's digital solutions provide dental practices and
laboratories with a choice of open digital technology that can be
integrated into the workflow providing patients with the best quality patient
care.

title: Communication between a dentist and the dental laboratory
transcript: one of the challenges with communicating and I talked earlier about the fact that you know the best communication is between an individual dentist an individual technician when the challenges with that is over time you develop a shorthand you develop a quick way of saying make this one look like this and make that one look like that that shorthand tends to be unique between the two of you or between a small group of people there are universal terms but the number of times somebody has asked me what does this mean because it's not a shorthand or it's not something they would use it in where they're from or how they were trained I see you D for somebody doesn't necessarily mean to see you nee for somebody else a byte block for somebody is a byte room for somebody else there's a whole bunch of things that become very specialized very quickly and very regional they were localized we all have our own preferences the how its expressed and so you may read a set of instructions you read a prescription from a dentist and not understand what they're looking for because they're using the correct words and now it's not using them in the way that you understand as a technician [Music]
